content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : William M. Chamberlain                    Date : July 2, 1981
    General Counsel
    California Energy Commission
    1111 Howe Avenue
    Sacramento, CA 95815


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION      A-81-07-033
       Diane Maura Fishburn
       Legal Division
Subject: Advice Request


     This memorandum is sent in response to your request for
comments on your opinion to Commissioner C. Suzanne Reed. Our
understanding of the facts from your opinion and our discussions
is as follows.

     Commissioner Reed's husband was recently employed by
Southern California Gas Company (SoCal), a California utility.
On June 17, 1981, the Energy Commission considered the adoption
of new residential building standards to conserve energy. We
understand from our meeting with you that SoCal supplied some
information in the development of these standards, but did not
actively work for or against their adoption.

     Our staff has considered this matter, and we do not agree
with the analysis that you used in reaching the conclusion that
Commissioner Reed does not have a conflict of interest. Therefore,
in this memorandum, I will set forth our analysis of the issues
involved.

     As you know, a public official is prohibited from partici-
pating in governmental decisions in which he or she has a
financial interest. Government Code Section 87100.1/ A financial
interest in a decision exists when it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect on the
investments or real property of the official or a source of income
to the official. Section 87103. Since the income to an official
includes the income to his or her spouse, SoCal is a source of
income to Commissioner Reed. Section 82030. Therefore, if the
decision in question were to have a material financial effect on
SoCal, Ms. Reed would be required to disqualify herself from
participating.


_______________________________

     1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

William M. Chamberlain                -2-                July 2, 1981


     As you pointed out in your opinion, any effects on SoCal
from the adoption of the building standards would be distin-
guishable from the effects on the public generally, and,
therefore, a potential for conflicts of interest does exist.

     Also, as you correctly surmised in your opinion, the
determinative issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
there will be a material financial effect on SoCal as a result
of the Energy Commission's decision on new residential building
standards.

     The Fair Political Practices Commission has determined
that the element of foreseeability is satisfied when there is
a substantial likelihood that there will be financial effects
on the entity in question; certainty is not required. Opinion
requested by Tom Thorner, 1 FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-680,
December 4, 1975). The intended and anticipated effects of a
governmental decision are also "reasonably foreseeable" within
the meaning of Section 87103. Opinion requested by Gary G.
Gilmore, 3 FPPC Opinions 38 (No. 76-089, April 6, 1977).

     A foreseeable financial effect must also be material before
an official must disqualify himself. The Commission has defined
materiality in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. Subsection (a)
of Section 18702 defines material financial effect as a "signi-
ficant" effect. Subsection (b) provides specific numerical
guidelines for analyzing materiality. In the event that the
specific guidelines cannot be applied, subsection (a) should be
used. Whichever subsection of Section 18702 is applied, the
resolution of the materiality or significance element is, as
you pointed out, "heavily dependent upon the specific facts
involved."

     In your analysis, you set forth a number of facts. Some of
these facts are relevant to the determination of materiality.
However, some factors which you relied upon cannot properly be
taken into account in determining materiality:

     1.    Whether or not potential financial benefits to SoCal
of natural gas conservation tend to balance or offset any
possible losses;

     2.    Whether or not any potential financial losses are taken
into account by the Public Utilities Commission in setting SoCal's
rates;

     3.    Whether or not SoCal's financial health or profitability
are threatened by the adoption of the standards.

William M. Chamberlain                -3-                    July 2, 1981


     The Political Reform Act focuses on the direct financial
effect of a decision, such as its effect on sales or on
expenditures on capital assets, and not on the decision's
ultimate effect on the profitability of the entire business
enterprise. The idea that a financial effect can be "offset"
by some other effect, by a response by SoCAl, or by the PUC
during the ratemaking process is inconsistent with the Act.
It is the direct effect on a business' sales, expenditures
and capital assets, and not whether the business can accomodate
that effect without loss of income, which determines whether
there is a conflict of interest.

     Applying this analysis to the present situation, and relying
on the facts provided to us by your office, we find the following
facts to be determinative on the issues of foreseeability and
materiality. The proposed standards only modify residential
conservation standards already in effect; the incremental
difference in the energy savings between the present and proposed
standards is relatively slight. Since the proposed standards
apply only to new housing, they will take effect gradually and
thus will not measurably affect SoCAl's longterm contracts with
its gas suppliers. In addition, the demand for natural gas exceeds
the supply so that even if the residential demand is reduced,
there will be no impact in total gas sales; in particular, electric
utilities who are classified by the PUC as "curtailable" or "D-5"
users have an on-going demand for natural gas. Even though PG
users pay a different rate than residential customers, this shift
of energy consumption from one class of users to another will not
affect SoCAl's total revenues form sales since, as you stated, the
PUC adjusts rates so the revenues remain essentially the same.
We also understand that the standards will not affect whether or
not SoCAl must consider increases or decreases in its expenditures
on capital assets. Assuming that the facts you provided to us are
true and complete, we have concluded that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the decision on residential building standards
will materially affect SoCAl. Therefore, Ms. Reed does not have
a financial interest within the meaning of the Political Reform
Act and would not be prohibited from participating in the decision.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible signature]

                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMP:qs

Suzanne Reed. Our understanding of the facts from your opinion and our discussions is as follows. Commissioner Reed's husband was recently employed by Southern California Gas Company (SoCal), a California utility. On June 17, 1981, the Energy Commission considered the adoption of new residential building standards to conserve energy. We understand from our meeting with you that SoCal supplied some information in the development of these standards, but did not actively work for or against their adoption. Our staff has considered this matter, and we do not agree with the analysis that you used in reaching the conclusion that Commissioner Reed does not have a conflict of interest. Therefore, in this memorandum, I will set forth our analysis of the issues involved. As you know, a public official is prohibited from participating in governmental decisions in which he or she has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87100.1/ A financial interest in a decision exists when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the investments or real property of the official or a source of income to the official. Section 87103. Since the income to an official includes the income to his or her spouse, SoCal is a source of income to Commissioner Reed. Section 82030. Therefore, if the decision in question were to have a material financial effect on SoCal, Ms. Reed would be required to disqualify herself from participating. 1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. As you pointed out in your opinion, any effects on SoCal from the adoption of the building standards would be distinguishable from the effects on the public generally, and, therefore, a potential for conflicts of interest does exist. Also, as you correctly surmised in your opinion, the determinative issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on SoCal as a result of the Energy Commission's decision on new residential building standards. The Fair Political Practices Commission has determined that the element of foreseeability is satisfied when there is a substantial likelihood that there will be financial effects on the entity in question; certainty is not required. Opinion requested by Tom Thorner, 1 FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-089, December 4, 1975). The intended and anticipated effects of a governmental decision are also "reasonably foreseeable" within the meaning of Section 87103. Opinion requested by Gary G. Gilmore, 3 FPPC Opinions 38 (No. 76-089, April 6, 1977). A foreseeable financial effect must also be material before an official must disqualify herself. The Commission has defined materiality in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. Subsection (a) of Section 18702 defines material financial effect as a "significant"
